entries.title: Inspector overview: breaking a message into parts
entries.body:
# Inspector - structured breakdown of HTTP messages

The **Inspector** is a side panel that shows HTTP and WebSocket messages not as raw text, but laid out into understandable parts: query string parameters separately, the body separately, cookies separately, headers separately. The main value is that you can quickly view and adjust the interesting elements of a message without jumping between tabs and digging through raw HTTP syntax by hand.

Where it appears: the **Inspector** lives in the side panel next to the **message editor** in many tools - in **Proxy** (on intercepted requests and in history), in **Repeater**, in **Intruder**, and in other places where Burp shows HTTP messages.

## What it can do

- **Auto-decoding.** Values are automatically decoded from URL, HTML, and Base64 encoding. A cookie or parameter that in raw form looks like a jumble of `%xx` and base64, the **Inspector** will show in readable form - you don't need to run the text through decoders by hand.
- **Editing without raw syntax.** You can add, delete, and reorder elements with buttons. You don't need to manually fix the rest of the request - Burp itself recalculates what depends on your edit (for example, the body length).
- **Editing in decoded form.** You change a value the way it reads; on send, Burp itself re-applies the needed sequence of encodings so the request goes out correctly.
- **Switching HTTP/1 <-> HTTP/2.** The **Inspector** can translate a request between protocol versions, automatically converting the equivalent parts (including the HTTP/2 pseudo-headers that start with a colon - `:method`, `:path`, `:authority`).
- **Analysis of a selected fragment.** Select any piece of text in the message editor - the **Inspector** will show its ASCII code points and automatic decoding. Convenient for figuring out what kind of non-printable character it is or how a substring is encoded.

## Which sections the message is broken into

The **Inspector** groups the data of the selected request-response pair by category. Depending on the message, these are:

- **Request attributes** - the request attributes: method, path, protocol version.
- **Query parameters** - the query string parameters (what's after `?`).
- **Body parameters** - the body parameters (form, JSON fields, etc.).
- **Request cookies** - the request cookies.
- **Request headers** - the request headers.
- **Response headers** - the response headers.
- **Response cookies** - the response cookies (what came in `Set-Cookie`).
- **HTTP/2 pseudo-headers** - the HTTP/2 pseudo-headers (with a colon at the start), when the request is over HTTP/2.

Each section shows an element counter and expands on click. Inside is a "name / value" table where the values are already decoded.

## Why this matters in practice

- **Quickly see all parameters at once.** Instead of hunting with your eyes for `id=`, `role=`, `token=` in a long request string, you see them in a neat list - it's immediately clear where the injection points are for IDOR, injections, role manipulation.
- **Break down a token/cookie.** A long base64 cookie unfolds into readable content; a JWT and encoded values become understandable without external utilities.
- **Cleanly add a header.** For example, append `X-Forwarded-For` or substitute a value - with a button, without risking breaking the request's formatting.
- **Convert a request to HTTP/2 (or back).** Sometimes a vulnerability (for example, a desync or a filter bypass) shows up only on a specific protocol version - a one-click switch.

> Tip: the **Inspector** and the raw editor show the same message, just from different angles. Structure and parameters are more convenient to view/edit in the **Inspector**, while fine manual work with raw bytes is better in the **Pretty/Raw/Hex** modes of the editor. They are in sync: an edit in one is reflected in the other.

> Pitfall: editing through the **Inspector** is possible only where the message is editable in principle (**Repeater**, intercept in **Proxy**, **Intruder**). In viewing modes (history, **Logger**) the **Inspector** works read-only - to change and resend, first send the request to **Repeater**.

entries.title: Inspector: getting started
entries.body:
# Getting started with the Inspector

This section is a quick start: where to find the **Inspector**, how to expand the sections, how to read decoded values, and how to catch the breakdown of a selected fragment.

## Where it is and how it opens

The **Inspector** is in the side panel next to the **message editor** on many Burp tabs - in **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Intruder**, etc. There's usually no need to separately "enable" it: as soon as you select a request-response pair, the **Inspector** in the side panel shows its content itself, grouped by category - headers, cookies, parameters.

If the panel is collapsed into a narrow strip at the edge, click it to expand. The behavior and look of the side panel are configured in **Side panel settings** (there you can also pin it open so it doesn't collapse).

## How to read

1. Select a request (in history, in intercept, in **Repeater**).
2. Sections will appear in the side panel: **Request attributes**, **Query parameters**, **Body parameters**, **Request cookies**, **Request headers**, and, if there's a response - **Response headers**, **Response cookies**. Each section has an element counter.
3. Click a section header to expand it and see the "name / value" table.
4. The values are already **decoded** for readability. To view a specific element in detail, expand it with the **>** arrow - Burp will show the decoded result (useful for long URL/Base64 values).

## Analysis of a selected fragment

A very convenient little thing: select any string right in the message editor - and the **Inspector** automatically decodes that selection the same way (URL/HTML/Base64) and shows its essence. This way you quickly understand what's encoded in a suspicious piece of a request, without opening external decoders.

## Editing values (where it's allowed)

If the message is editable (for example, in **Repeater**):

1. Expand the needed section and find the element.
2. Fix its value right in the **Inspector** field (in decoded form - the way it reads).
3. Click **Apply changes** - Burp will re-apply the needed sequence of encodings and update the request.

Elements can be not only edited but also **added, deleted, and reordered** with buttons - and you don't have to manually put the rest of the request in order, Burp does it itself (in detail - in the entry on editing requests).

## Recommended first pass

```
1. Open Repeater (or select a request in HTTP history)
2. Expand the side panel -> Inspector
3. Expand Query parameters / Body parameters -> view all injection points as a list
4. Expand a long cookie with the > arrow -> read it decoded
5. Select a piece in the editor -> look at its decode/ASCII in the Inspector
```

> Tip: keep the **Inspector** pinned open (via **Side panel settings**) - when breaking down an unfamiliar API this is the fastest way to see the whole request structure at once, without scrolling through raw text.

> Pitfall: in "viewer" tools (history, **Logger**) the **Inspector** fields are read-only. Don't try to change anything there - first send the request to **Repeater**, and edit it there.

entries.title: Inspector: editing requests
entries.body:
# Editing requests through the Inspector

When a message is editable (**Repeater**, intercept in **Proxy**, **Intruder**), the **Inspector** lets you change a request part by part - add, delete, reorder, and edit attributes, parameters, cookies, and headers without touching the raw HTTP syntax. Burp itself recalculates everything dependent (value encoding, body length), so the request stays correct.

## Add an element

1. Expand the needed section (**Query parameters**, **Body parameters**, **Request cookies**, **Request headers**, etc.).
2. At the bottom of the section click **Add**.
3. Enter a name and value.
4. Click **Add** again to confirm.

This way, for example, you add a test header (`X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1`), a new parameter to check for hidden logic, or mix in an extra cookie.

## Delete an element

Select an element and click the **trash icon**. Several elements can be selected by dragging with the mouse (click-and-drag) and deleted at once.

## Reorder

Select an element and move it up/down with the **arrow buttons** at the bottom of the section. The order of parameters and headers sometimes matters - for example, when checking how the server reacts to duplicate parameters (HTTP Parameter Pollution).

## Change a name or value

Double-click an entry in the **Inspector** and edit the name or value in place. Burp will automatically apply the same sequence of encodings to your change, so in the raw request the value ends up correctly encoded.

## Insert a line break (CRLF)

Needed for checks like CRLF injection / HTTP response splitting. Click the arrow next to an element, place the cursor in the **Name** or **Value** field, and press **Shift + Return** - the carriage return and line break characters (CR LF) will be inserted.

## Insert a non-printable character (for example, a null byte)

Two ways:

- **Via the Selection widget.** Add any placeholder character, select it, and via the **Selection** widget in the **Inspector** change its code point - for example, to `00` for a null byte.
- **Via Hex.** Or switch to the **Hex** tab of the message editor and write the needed byte there directly.

This is useful for bypasses sensitive to the null byte and control characters.

## Copy an element

Select elements and in the context menu choose **Copy name** or **Copy value**. Important: the data is copied in its **original (encoded) form**, not decoded - that is, you get exactly what will actually go out in the request.

## Where you can edit and where you can't

The **Inspector**'s editing features are available in both **Professional** and **Community Edition** - but only next to an **editable** message editor. That is:

- **You can edit:** **Repeater**, an intercepted request in **Proxy > Intercept**, positions in **Intruder**.
- **View only:** **Proxy > HTTP history**, **Logger**, any retrospective tables. There the **Inspector** shows the structure but doesn't let you change it - first send the request to **Repeater**.

## A practical scenario (IDOR via the Inspector)

```
1. Found a request with an id parameter in HTTP history -> Send to Repeater (Ctrl+R)
2. In Repeater open the Inspector -> Query parameters (or Body parameters)
3. Double-click the id value -> substitute someone else's identifier
4. (opt.) Add -> add X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 if you hit an IP filter
5. Send -> compare the response: if someone else's data came back -> a candidate for IDOR
```

> Tip: editing through the **Inspector** is good because Burp doesn't let you "break" the request - the length and encoding are recalculated automatically. This especially helps with JSON bodies and complex parameters, where manual editing in raw form easily falls apart.

> Pitfall: remember the auto-encoding. If you write an already-encoded string into a value, the **Inspector** will encode it once more. When you need full control over the raw bytes (exact payloads, tricky encoding), edit in the **Raw/Hex** mode of the editor, not in the **Inspector**.
